title: Flask Routing – Mapping URLs to Functions

meta.title: Flask Routing Guide – Dynamic URLs, Methods & Converters (2026)
meta.description: Master URL routing in Flask. Learn how to handle dynamic paths, use variable converters (int, string, uuid), and manage HTTP methods for RESTful APIs.

content.value: Routing is the process of binding a URL to a specific Python function. In Flask, this is handled primarily through the `@app.route()` decorator. When a user visits a specific path, Flask identifies the associated function (called a 'View Function') and executes it to return a response.
Static routes are simple, but dynamic routes allow you to capture values from the URL and pass them as arguments to your function. This is vital for resources like user profiles or blog posts.

By default, dynamic segments are treated as strings. You can use converters to ensure the data is of a specific type (int, float, path, uuid) before the function is even called. If the type doesn't match, Flask returns a 404 error.
By default, routes only respond to GET requests. To build a RESTful API, you must explicitly list the methods the route should handle.

Never hardcode URLs in your application. Use `url_for()` to generate URLs based on the function name. This ensures that if you change the URL path in the decorator, all your links automatically update.

from flask import url_for

# Generates '/user/Kishore'
url = url_for('show_user_profile', username='Kishore')

faq.question: What is the difference between /path and /path/ (Trailing Slashes)?
faq.answer: If a route is defined as /path/, visiting /path will result in a 301 redirect to /path/. If defined as /path, visiting /path/ will result in a 404 error. It is best practice to include the trailing slash for 'folder-like' URLs.

faq.question: Can one function have multiple routes?
What are Blueprints in Flask routing?
faq.answer: Blueprints are a way to organize your application into distinct components (e.g., auth, admin, api). They allow you to define routes in separate files and group them under a common URL prefix.

quiz.question: Which converter would you use to capture a URL that contains multiple slashes (e.g., /uploads/images/logo.png)?
